metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace every reference to `rebar fsck --fix` in the plan (What section and AC3) with `rebar fsck --repair`, which is the actual existing CLI flag confirmed at `fsck.py:682` and `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out`.
findings.finding: The plan's hint text (What section) and AC3 both direct users to run `rebar fsck --fix`, but `--fix` is not a valid CLI flag. The actual flag is `--repair` — confirmed by `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` line 1 (`[--repair [--dry-run] [--limit=N]]`) and `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py:682` (`do_repair = "--repair" in argv`). A user who runs `rebar fsck --fix` will receive no error but no repair either (the flag is silently ignored), making the hint actively misleading.
findings.location: What section ('Emit a WARNING pointing to `rebar fsck --fix`'); AC3 ('Hint points to `rebar fsck --fix`')
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'rebar fsck --fix' → 'rebar fsck --repair' in the What section and AC3. If WS3 intends to add a `--fix` alias, that should be stated explicitly as a WS3 deliverable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the hint command from `rebar fsck --fix` to `rebar fsck --repair` everywhere in the plan (What section and AC3), matching the actual flag at `fsck.py:682`.
findings.finding: The plan directs the user-facing hint to point to `rebar fsck --fix`, but this flag does not exist. The actual mutating flag on `fsck` is `--repair` (confirmed at `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py:682`: `do_repair = "--repair" in argv`). `--fix` is a flag only on `rebar validate`, not `rebar fsck`. An implementer following the plan will embed a broken CLI command in the emitted WARNING.
findings.location: What section / AC3: 'Hint points to `rebar fsck --fix`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace every occurrence of `rebar fsck --fix` in the plan with `rebar fsck --repair`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `rebar fsck --fix` with `rebar fsck --repair` everywhere in the plan (What section and AC3) to match the actual CLI flag verified in `src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt`.
findings.finding: The hint the plan emits points to `rebar fsck --fix`, but `--fix` does not exist as a CLI flag. The actual mutating flag is `--repair` (confirmed by `src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt` line 1: `Usage: rebar fsck [--output json] [--repair [--dry-run] [--limit=N]]` and `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py:682`). A user who follows the hint will receive a CLI error.
findings.location: What section / AC3: "Hint points to `rebar fsck --fix`"
findings.suggested_fix: Change every occurrence of `rebar fsck --fix` in the plan to `rebar fsck --repair`. The flag `--repair` is the existing mutating remediation path (implemented at `fsck.py:682`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `gen_env_registry.py`'s AST drift gate does NOT auto-document config-section env overrides (they're dynamically derived, not literal strings); specify that `REBAR_ENSURE_HINT_INTERVAL_SECS` and `REBAR_ENSURE_HINT_ENABLED` must be manually added to `docs/env-vars.md` (or that `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS` / the generator must be extended to cover the `_canonical_env_name()` indirection).
findings.finding: The plan claims that routing `ensure.hint_interval_secs` and `ensure.hint_enabled` through `_config_schema` + the env registry 'passes the env-registry drift gate (`gen_env_registry.py`)'. This is mechanically imprecise: `gen_env_registry.py` is an AST literal-string scanner — it catches only direct `os.environ["X"]` / `os.environ.get("X", ...)` / `os.getenv("X", ...)` calls and calls to the helper functions in `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS`. Config-section env overrides are resolved dynamically through `_canonical_env_name()` iteration over `_SECTIONS` — no literal `REBAR_ENSURE_*` string ever appears in source. As a result, the two new `REBAR_ENSURE_*` env vars will be functionally correct (the config machinery picks them up) but will NOT appear in `docs/env-vars.md` and the drift gate will not fail if they are undocumented. The plan's 'drift gate' claim gives implementers false assurance about what the gate enforces for config-section keys.
findings.location: What section — 'Interval + suppression are TYPED CONFIG routed through `_config_schema` + the env registry (NOT a raw os.environ read — there is a CI drift gate `gen_env_registry.py`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the parenthetical claim with: 'routed through `_config_schema` + env layer (the canonical env names `REBAR_ENSURE_HINT_INTERVAL_SECS` and `REBAR_ENSURE_HINT_ENABLED` must be manually added to `docs/env-vars.md` — the drift gate covers only literal-string `os.environ` reads, not config-section indirection)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/config.py` (re-export), and the `Config` dataclass, `_SECTION_CLASSES`, and `_SECTIONS` table inside `_config_schema.py` as explicit required edit targets when adding the new `ensure` config section.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/config.py` is an unenumerated edit target. It re-exports every dataclass from `_config_schema` via explicit `from rebar._config_schema import XConfig as XConfig` lines. Adding a new `EnsureConfig` section to `_config_schema.py` requires adding a corresponding `from rebar._config_schema import EnsureConfig as EnsureConfig` line to `config.py`, plus updating `Config`, `_SECTION_CLASSES`, and `_SECTIONS` inside `_config_schema.py` itself. The plan names only `_config_schema` as the edit target.
findings.location: What section: 'Interval + suppression are TYPED CONFIG routed through `_config_schema` + the env registry'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify what happens when the `.ensure-hinted` write fails: either (a) accept warn-once-per-invocation in that session and document it, or (b) fall back to an in-process flag so the rate-limit degrades to per-process rather than spamming every write; either way, log at INFO/WARNING (not just debug) when the timestamp file itself cannot be written.
findings.finding: If `.ensure-hinted` is persistently unwritable (permissions, read-only FS, full disk), every write-path hook invocation that finds `pending=true` will re-emit the WARNING because the rate-limit file can never be updated. The exception is swallowed at debug level, making this failure invisible to operators; users experience warning spam on every mutation.
findings.location: What section — `.ensure-hinted` atomic timestamp write; AC line: 'The hook swallows all its own exceptions including lazy-import failures (debug-logged)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an in-process 'already hinted this process' boolean that is set to true after the first WARNING emission, regardless of whether the `.ensure-hinted` file write succeeds. This degrades gracefully: if the file is unwritable, hints are suppressed for the remainder of the process (one warning per process, not one per write).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate the `_config_schema.py` scaffolding as a deliverable: add `EnsureConfig` dataclass with `hint_interval_secs: int = 86400` and `hint_enabled: bool = True`, register it in `_SECTION_CLASSES`/`_SECTIONS`, add the `ensure: EnsureConfig` field to `Config`, and re-export from `config.py`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `ensure.hint_interval_secs` and `ensure.hint_enabled` are 'typed CONFIG routed through `_config_schema`' as if these keys can simply be added to an existing config section, but no `ensure` config section or `EnsureConfig` dataclass exists anywhere in `_config_schema.py`. The exhaustive section list is: `verify`, `ticket`, `ticket_clarity`, `compact`, `sync`, `mcp`, `reconciler`, `jira`, `scratch`, `tracker`. Creating a new `ensure` section requires: (a) a new `@dataclass EnsureConfig`, (b) an entry in `_SECTION_CLASSES`, (c) an entry in `_SECTIONS` with coercer lambdas for both keys, (d) an `ensure: EnsureConfig` field on `Config`, and (e) a re-export from `config.py`. None of this scaffolding work is enumerated as a plan deliverable.
findings.location: What section ('Interval + suppression are TYPED CONFIG routed through `_config_schema` + the env registry'); AC3 ('`ensure.hint_interval_secs` (default 86400) and `ensure.hint_enabled` (default true) with a registered `REBAR_*` env override')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Config scaffold' bullet to the What section: 'Add EnsureConfig dataclass to _config_schema.py (hint_interval_secs: int = 86400, hint_enabled: bool = True), wire into _SECTION_CLASSES/_SECTIONS/_Config, re-export from config.py, and add REBAR_ENSURE_* to the env registry so the gen_env_registry drift gate passes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a WS2-owned proving step for AC5: create a fixture store whose `.ensure-applied` contains the full current registry id set, invoke a mutation via the hook, and assert no WARNING is emitted — removing the sole reliance on a WS1 cross-reference.
findings.finding: AC5 ('A fresh auto-init'd worktree never hints; cross-reference WS1 for that guarantee') delegates the proving guarantee entirely to WS1 with no WS2-owned end-to-end check. If WS1's init writes a stale or partial `.ensure-applied`, the 'never hints on fresh init' invariant silently breaks — but WS2 has no criterion that exercises this boundary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command to each AC: (AC1) a unit test asserting `open()` is called at most once across N hook invocations; (AC2) a grep/import-graph command confirming no marker read on read-path modules; (AC3) a shell sequence that sets `.ensure-hinted` to `epoch+1`, fires a mutation, asserts WARNING, re-fires, asserts silence; (AC4) `python -c 'import event_append'` succeeds with a deliberately broken `ensures.py`; (AC5) a scenario where `.ensure-applied` == full registry set triggers a mutation with no WARNING emitted.
findings.finding: None of the five ACs include a concrete proving command. An executor must invent all verification steps, creating risk of green-but-hollow sign-off: e.g., AC1 ('cached boolean; ≤1 file read per process') needs a unit test asserting the underlying file is opened exactly once across multiple hook invocations; AC2 needs a grep or import-graph check confirming no marker read on read-path entry points; AC3 needs a command that exercises the rate-limit sentinel file (e.g., set `.ensure-hinted` to an old mtime, invoke a mutation, assert WARNING is emitted; then re-invoke and assert no second WARNING); AC4 needs `python -c 'import event_append'` (or equivalent) to assert no ImportError from a lazy-import boundary; AC5 needs a scenario where `.ensure-applied` contains the full registry set and a mutation is triggered, asserting no hint is emitted.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CLAUDE.md / ADR / design-doc entry capturing: (a) the write-path hook pattern and why pure-read paths are excluded, (b) the inline-committer gap as an accepted tradeoff, (c) the `.ensure-hinted` file's ownership and semantics, and (d) the `ensure.*` config namespace rationale.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — hooking the write path for a pending-ensures nudge, a new `.ensure-hinted` marker file, and a new `ensure.*` config namespace — but mentions no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture it. The 'inline-committer gap accepted' decision is also a non-obvious deliberate tradeoff that future maintainers need to find.
findings.location: ## What / ## Scope / AC2 ('the inline-committer gap is documented as accepted')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short section to CLAUDE.md (or a new `docs/adr/WS2-ensure-hint.md`) covering: the hook's placement rationale, the intentional exclusion of inline-committing paths (compact/txn/transition_close/delete) and why it is acceptable for a best-effort nudge, the `.ensure-hinted` ownership contract (WS2-owned, git-ignored, atomic timestamp), and the `ensure.*` config namespace defaults and override mechanism.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the thread-safety strategy for cache initialization (e.g., a module-level lock around the first read, `once`-style initialization, or explicit acknowledgment that a benign double-read race is acceptable with a note that the result is idempotent).
findings.finding: The per-process cache of the `pending` boolean is described as 'cached once/proc' but the plan does not specify a thread-safety mechanism for cache initialization. In a multi-threaded server process (e.g., Puma/Sidekiq workers sharing a process), two threads can concurrently find the cache uninitialized, both read `.ensure-applied`, and both set the cache — a benign TOCTOU, but the spec leaves this undefined.
findings.location: What section — 'Compute this ONCE per process and cache the boolean'; AC line 1: 'computed once per process (cached boolean)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add testing callouts for the three key behavioral contracts: (a) hook swallows all exceptions including lazy-import failures without raising (failure-path test); (b) compute-once cache results in ≤1 file read per process even across multiple write calls (cache boundary test); (c) rate-limit boundary — hint fires at/after `hint_interval_secs` and is suppressed before it (edge-case test).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new user-facing behavioral contract (writes never fail due to the hook; hint is rate-limited; suppression works) but specifies no testing strategy — no failure-path tests (lazy-import failure swallowed, `.ensure-hinted` write failure, malformed `.ensure-applied`), no rate-limit boundary tests (at-interval, just-before, just-after), and no test for the compute-once cache guarantee. The fail-silent contract in particular is a new guarantee that could silently regress.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (fail-silent) and AC1 (cached once per process)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Testing' section or annotate ACs with test expectations: e.g., AC4 → 'test: inject ImportError into the hook and assert the surrounding write completes normally'; AC1 → 'test: mock file read and assert it is called exactly once across N write-path invocations in one process'; AC3 → 'test: set .ensure-hinted to now−(interval+1s) and assert hint fires; set to now−(interval−1s) and assert suppressed'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `ensure.hint_interval_secs` and `ensure.hint_enabled` need their own `[ensure]` config section, or whether they can be flat fields in an existing section (e.g., `tracker` or `verify`) matching the precedent of `enable_code_review`, `progressive_drift_refresh`, and `remediation_window_minutes` in `VerifyConfig`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new top-level `ensure` config section (with keys `hint_interval_secs` and `hint_enabled`) routed through `_config_schema`, but analogous boolean toggles and interval scalars in the codebase live as flat fields in already-existing sections (e.g., `enable_code_review`, `progressive_drift_refresh`, `remediation_window_minutes` all live in `VerifyConfig`) rather than in their own dedicated sections. Creating `EnsureConfig` + adding it to `Config`, `_SECTION_CLASSES`, `_SECTIONS`, and `config.py` re-exports for only two keys is config-surface proliferation with a single consumer (the write-path hook) — violating both the Rule-of-Three (one consumer, no existing call-sites for `ensure.*`) and the no-new-section-for-one-consumer principle.
findings.location: What section / AC3: 'ensure.hint_interval_secs (default 86400) and ensure.hint_enabled (default true) with a registered REBAR_* env override'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `hint_interval_secs: int = 86400` and `hint_enabled: bool = True` as flat fields in `TrackerConfig` (since the hint is tracker-lifecycle-related) or `VerifyConfig`, avoiding a new section entirely. Update `_SECTIONS["tracker"]` with the two new coercers. This matches codebase precedent and eliminates the new-section overhead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit merge-ordering pre-condition to the plan stating WS2 requires WS1's `ensures.py` to be present and callable before WS2 can be integrated.
findings.finding: `ensures.py` (the WS1 registry the plan reads `available_ids` from) does not exist anywhere in the codebase. The plan treats it as a WS1 deliverable but does not state any guard or pre-condition ensuring WS2 cannot be merged before WS1 lands. An implementer starting WS2 before WS1 ships will have a missing import target.
findings.location: What section: 'available_ids = the ids in `ensures.py`'s registry'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
